speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Anita White. Today's date is August 27, 1995. I am interviewing survivor Mrs. Ida Paluch. The interview is conducted in Skokie, Illinois, USA. The language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please give us your name and spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ida Kersz. I-D-A K-E-R-S-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ida Paluch. Pronounced the same way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-D-A, last name, P-A-L-U-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please give us that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember my next name after Ida Paluch was Irena Maj.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Irena, I-R-E-N-A. Maj, M-A-J.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was May 3, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: My age presently is 56.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the city in which you were born, and the country please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Sosnowiec, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since you were born just a few months before the war started, let us begin with the earliest memories that you have of yourself, and events which you remember. Would you please, wherever you can, give us the names of the places, and the dates, whatever you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: My first memory comes to me when I was about three and a half years old in Sosnowiec ghetto in 1941. Actually, had to be a little later, 1942, when we were standing outdoors with other people, big crowd, and people were being selected. And I remember that my mother, who was taken away from us-- or we were taken away from her-- ran in front of us to a building. And I ran after her. My brother and my sister, older sister, Genia, and my brother, Adam, we ran after her. She ran into a building in a big entrance to the stairway. And as she ran there, with panic, we were probably crying. And she ran into stairway, first to the first floor. And there was a closet. And she figured out that was a mistake, so she ran upstairs, higher. And then she ran to a window. We stood there, and she disappeared through the window. She jumped to her death.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were left then with your brother and Genia, and with whom else?

speaker: interviewee
text: As it happened, as, as much as I remember, my aunt Rozia took us under her wing. And she took me and my brother, Adam, and my sister, Genia, with her own little son, Abus, into a hiding. The only thing I remember at that time is on the day, when she met Mr. Wilhelm Maj, and he came from Czestochowa to visit her. And he came to the ghetto because he was sent by the neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews didn't live in their own homes anymore. He came to the ghetto, and as luck would have it, my aunt walked through with me. I was hanging onto her skirt. So he ask her, who is this little girl? Because he knew her from before. He knew she had a son. And she explained, that this is my niece, and probably an orphan. And this is an extra mouth to feed. He responded that he would take me with him under the condition that she will never see me again. If he takes me, I'm his. And-- May I ask you, you said he was-- was he inside the ghetto or outside the ghetto? Was there a, a barrier, a wall--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a wall, or a barbed wire? How was the ghetto arranged?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, at the place where we were standing, it was not far from a gate. And it was a wire. And my aunt handed me over the wire to him. And I believe it was already quite late in the evening. That's when she did it, not during the daylight.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how-- did she actually contact him, or how did he know about you? Please explain that.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was her business associate. Before the war, she had a store. And he brought to her all kind of merchandise. And when he came the last time, he came to her house. And the neighbors told him, there are no Jews left here. If you want to find her, go to the ghetto. That's how.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father at that time? And by the way, tell us your father's name and his occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Chaim Liezor Paluch. Many times he was called by friends and relatives Leon, Leon. His profession is tailor, and I understand that was the profession of our grandfather on both sides.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he then when you were just with your mother and aunt? Where was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father told me that the minute the war broke out, he volunteered to the Polish army to fight, to defend Poland. And we just didn't see him anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him immediately as he joined the Polish army? Do you have an idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he told me later on that he was taken prisoner by the Germans. And when they were by the border by Russia, they were taken prisoners by the Russians together with the Germans. And he ended up in the Asian part of Russia, working there almost like a slave in coal mines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I would like to bring you back to the moment where Mr. Maj is taking-- Wilhelm Maj is taking you from your aunt Rozia-- only you. Do you recall what-- where was Genia and your brother Adam at the time when you were given to the Maj?

speaker: interviewee
text: They probably were at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only one-- In the ghetto. I was the only one walking with my aunt at that time. And Mr. Maj, when he took me, it was winter. He put me in his own overcoat, took me on a train. And in the evening, late evening, we arrived to Czestochowa. And he introduced me to his wife when she opened the door. And he said, Jozefa, I brought you a Christmas gift. And when he opened the coat, and she saw me, she was really excited. She hugged me and kissed me. And the next thing that I remember is waking up at night, and they were decorating a Christmas tree.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she know-- Jozefa knew that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did explain to her that I am Jewish, that this has to be a family secret. And they discussed between themselves how they're going to explain my being there to the neighbors and to friends. And they came out with an idea that they're going to tell that I was their out of wedlock child, hidden in some kind of a village with relatives. And now that they got married, they brought me with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember the trimming of the Christmas tree? Did you get out of bed? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was just amazed looking at this beautiful, shining tree with a star with an angel on top. And I was just sitting there, and they told me, go to sleep. Tomorrow you'll have another surprise. And as I woke up next day, Mr. Maj was dressed as Santa Claus and brought in some gifts for me. And as he came in, my mother told me that I did recognize his voice. I said, this is not Santa, this is daddy. So I spoiled their surprise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you call them on the next day, Daddy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Immediately I called them mom and dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ask you to call them that? Or did you just as you-- just naturally?

speaker: interviewee
text: They introduced themselves as mother and dad. I also had a godmother and a godfather because in the next few days, they did christen me in the cathedral of Czestochowa in Katedralna, where I lived later on. And I remember having a beautiful silk dress, long dress and being baptized by getting wet my hair, and then taken to a photographer, I believe. And that's about it, as I remember from those early days about the change of my person to Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a name right then and then? Did you then change to-- were you changed-- what was your name then? Then it was my name, Irena. I think they kept my first initial from Ida into Irena.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you said that you remember your mother disappearing from in the window during the Aktion. But yet, when you came in a, a few months later, you were able to call this woman and that man mother and father. Mm-hmm. At that age, I did not understand that you're supposed to have only one mother. I thought this is natural to have other people called mom, dad because I was already in third hands. First I was with my aunt, with my natural mother, I mean, and then with those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then to your adoptive father? What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Polish father used to take a horse and a wagon and two cousin came with him. And they drove all over villages in the neighborhood of Czestochowa. And one night we got a messenger coming to our house telling that all three of them were shot by Gestapo, that were caught with the merchandise they were selling. But lately, when I went back to Czestochowa, this year, I found out that my Polish father was, was a partisan. And under the produce, he was carrying ammunition. And that's why they were all killed in the village of Odusz. And then I also found out through people who knew about this happening that the village was burned. And some people were burned in the church-- alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in reprisal for the activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother find out about your father's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was somebody coming to the house telling us that we better move out of there. And overnight, we moved into the father-in-law and mother-in-law of my Polish mother where we stayed till about when we were freed from the Germans. So we stayed in with grandpa and grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your mother, were you alone? Or did-- were-- did you have an addition to the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looks like during the time when my mother took me into her house, she was pregnant. And right after the death of her husband, a few months later, my mother had a baby which we named after our father, Wilhelm, Wilhelmina. And we moved in all with the-- our grandparents. The terrible thing was that the grandfather, Mr. Maj, he took his only son's death very hard to his heart. And he started to drink alcohol. And then he went almost crazy. He didn't stop drinking alcohol. He used to come and demolish the apartment. He used to kick everything, break everything. He took a credenza of dishes and threw it on the floor. And in his anger, he used to kick us and beat us. And sometimes in nightgowns, we were to run out, just to save our own flesh and blood because he was crazy. He even fed his horses with alcohol, a dog that ran away later. One day he put me in a barn with a horse that was drunk-- because he gave him alcohol-- and left me there overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, the adoptive mother, did not have any defenses against him.

speaker: interviewee
text: No she was really afraid of him, because he was the one who supported us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have wife? Was there a--

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a wife, which he did not respect. I don't know for what reason, but I think the most he was punishing his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well then, what did you and your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother saw the situation, and just to get away from this drunkard, she took me with her on trains. We took a luggage, and many times, whatever was possible to find, we would take on the train, like cigarettes, tobacco, alcohol. And we would sell it in the train. And many times, when times were really, really bad, my mother used to put half alcohol, half water, seal it with wax and put a little penny on it so it will look like it was never touched. And we sold it on the trains. But eventually, people would recognize us, so we had to change the routes. But my mother never gave up. We still did the same thing over and over because if not, we wouldn't be able to eat. We had, there were times when my mother, and I went through the neighbor's garbage and picked up the peels from the garbage to cook them. And I have to mention that my mother was really a just person. Whatever she gave her daughter, she gave the same to me. If she had a piece of bread, it was always cut evenly and distributed to her and to me. And I remember when we were hungry, both of us were crying. She said, children don't cry, go to sleep. You'll forget that you're hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- how was she feeding such a small baby on those impossible foods? What-- how was she able to organize it? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just by selling all her furniture, her jewelery. Whatever was in the house was no more there. We were probably the poorest family in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the father-in-law have no means? Or was he just so bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had means in the beginning. If he wouldn't drink, he was a very rich man. And when I-- the time I joined the family, he owned a lot of property in Czestochowa. He had carriages by the train station with people who worked for him. He had a carriage for a funeral, I remember, a black one. And a white carriage for weddings. He had a restaurant, a billiard place, and a laundromat. But eventually with him drinking and drinking, he lost at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wasn't that actually dangerous, too? Wasn't that black market, what you were doing, selling those things on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very dangerous because Gestapo was always on the trains looking for Jews. And they were looking through our luggages, checking what we have. And really, selling cigarettes was a no-no. And one day we were caught, both of us, my mother and I, because when they asked whose luggage it is, my mother turned away. And I said, it's ours because I was a child. And we were both taken off from the train to the Gestapo right in Czestochowa. And we were separated. I wasn't scared yet, because I didn't know why. But my mother was really scared. And we were separated. And the man who interviewed me there, he had a big bureau with pictures with his own family pictures. And he sat me across from him. And he looked at me, did a few gestures, picked up the phone. And he took me outside and there was my mother and he gave us back our luggage and yelled at as. He said, don't think that somebody else will be as nice as I am. Don't  you do that ever again because next time when we find you, this is not going to be the end. You're going to be punished. But we still did the same thing, I remember. Towards almost the end of the war, we were selling merchandise on trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably between my age four and six. For two years, we were schlepping on those trains, day and night and staying with strangers, sleeping over, I don't know, probably people that she knew from before. Or just people on the train, she would ask where can we sleep over, and we slept with strange people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took care of little Wilhelmina while you were traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandma. Grandma did. Grandpa and grandma did. Grandpa, I don't think so, but grandma, for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that last? How long did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did it for the longest time, till the end of the war, probably 1944 or '45. Overnight, we saw the change because German soldiers and Gestapo, overnight they disappeared from our town. And children were running around barefoot and running through the offices of the Germans, and picking up whatever there was. And there were rumors. Let's go here. Let's go there. There is sugar. There is, you know, chocolate. There is whatever. And I ran with a horde of children, barefoot, whatever, because I was always barefoot and schlepping my sister with me. I was her defender, and I was carrying her in my hands. And that was the end. And I remember also that the tanks came into the town, and people were really celebrating, and drinking, and singing. And the soldiers were very happy, picking up children, and kissing them. And I remember the first thing I tasted was saccharine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those were the Russians--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian soldiers, right--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russian Army. And they were good to the population as they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as to the children, they were very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they establish immediately a government in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. And the minute they did establish some kind of order, suddenly Jewish people were coming out probably right away, from the ghetto in Czestochowa. And there were some other places that they were following probably the soldiers. And the Jews right away organized themselves in a community with a president.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czestochowa. I just have to mention something before I go on that during those years, during the war, I was really very afraid of Jewish people. I was brought up as an anti-Semite. But not by my Polish mother. It was by the neighbors that constantly told stories about Jews. The Jews catch Polish children and use their blood for matzo. And the Jews have terrible big noses and long nails. And all those horror stories made me very afraid of the Jews that I'd never seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it make you hate the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or just be afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Afraid and hate. They told me that Jesus was killed by Jews. And I was a very much traditional Catholic child. I went to the-- to the church every Sunday, every holiday to pray. And I heard that Jesus was killed by the Jewish people. And for sure, I hated the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now there is a community, a Jewish community, established in Czestochowa. What happens to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my mother found out about the Jewish community, she went there asked them for an apartment. And she explained that she had a Jewish child during the war. And could they help us out because it's impossible to live with the father-in-law who is constantly drunk and crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she take you with you to the Jewish community when she went to tell them that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did. She took me with her. And when they look at me and figure out what age I am, in 1945, '46, they told her OK, we'll help you out. We'll give you an apartment. But in exchange, you'll let Ida-- or Irena-- go attend a Hebrew school once a week. So she agreed. And on a Friday night, I believe, she took me to this place where they supposedly had a school. And now I just decided it was not a school, but a synagogue. That's where my mother took me. And over there, there were Jewish people. Some had already beards. And some were with dark clothes. And they were praying in a different way than I was used to it. And then they spoke a language I did not understand. And when I thought about it, I thought oh my god. Those must be the Jews. Those are the terrible Jews. I was so scared that I decided that the minute I have a chance, I'll run away from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask your mother-- what was your reaction when your mother took you to the Jewish community and explained that you are a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did not explain at that time that I'm a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, but didn't she say she saved the Jewish child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she presents you as the Jewish child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She talked to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did not--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just sitting on a side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did not know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not, not know that she's talking about me. I thought, this is my mother. But then in my memory, I had another family. And at that time, I could not sort out what is it that I have had a mother, I have a mother. I did not understand that I had two mothers, and two families. I did not know the connection. But in my dreams, I did see other children with me, like my brother and my sister. So I was aware that there was somebody else in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now let me take you back. You came that Friday with your Polish mother, Jozefa, to the synagogue. What happens then? Then you realize you are the Jewish child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, I did not. I still thought this is a mistake. I am Christian. I did not realize anything, and I thought that my mother betrayed me. That's what I felt, that she gave me away to the Jews, maybe for money. I didn't know what was happening. But I saw that those are different people. They did not behave as Catholics. And I was so petrified. And the lady who was supposed to take me to the train station, she was pregnant. She wanted to do a mitzvah at that time, as I realized later. And she took me on a carriage and horse that was the taxi to the train station. And as she went out with me, toward the evening, I started to scream. People, help, help! Jews are taking me for matzo. And that was right probably after the Kielce pogrom because some people stopped us on the street, took sticks, and wanted to beat up the woman. And police came, and the minute she let my hand go, I ran away to the grandfather. I preferred to go to the grandfather now than to my mother because I felt she betrayed me. And I went straight to his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as I came there, he said, oh yes? That's she wanted to do? Probably take money for you. And he said, don't worry. I'll take care of you. While this all was happening, the Jewish people in Poland heard about this almost pogrom that I caused in the city of Czestochowa because people at that time were giving used to each other. They were looking for each other. And somehow in few days, everybody in Poland knew that I caused some kind of a disturbance in that city. Please describe that disturbance. You said they attacked the woman. But what else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. I ran away from there. So I don't know what happened to that woman after that. I don't know what resulted out of it. I believe that police stopped it. That's my belief because that was it. And at the same time, when this rumor was going around, my aunt and uncle, who came from Siberia-- my aunt Roma and my uncle-- when they heard the news-- my uncle told me later-- Roma said, that sounds like our Ida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they when they heard the news?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in Sosnowiec. And they came to Czestochowa to check me out when they heard that this is possible that it's Ida. Because I guess my aunt, before she died, she told anybody, and everybody in the family that one child maybe will survive this war. And will be one of the twins. So they came, and they looked at me from far, and they told me later, that I did look like my sister at the same time because when they knew my sister, she was about seven, eight. And when they saw me after the war, I was about the same age. And they saw the resemblance. At the same time, my own father came back from Russia. And when he came, he went straight to Sosnowiec to see who is-- who else is there. And he found out that he lost his wife and children. But there was still this rumor that one of the twins is still alive in Czestochowa. And when he met with Uncle Joe and Aunt Roma, they told him, you have to go to Czestochowa and check out this child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1946. So he did come.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I take you back for a moment? You said Aunt Rozia, the one who was-- the one who was left with you and gave you away to the Majs did not survive. Will you please tell us what happened to that Aunt Rozia and her family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, Aunt Rozia had the same end, end as my mother. She was walked from Auschwitz to Germany, at the end of the war, almost. And it was a bloody walk. People told me a lot about it. And Aunt Rozia hanged herself on a tree.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was the death march. Two witnesses after the war told me about her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to her child?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know what happened to her child. There were rumors that he was given away too. There were rumors that Genia was taken-- given away too. Some of my aunts later said-- I hate to repeat it, but they said, maybe everybody was taken away and left on the street. I'm the only one who survived because I was blond and a girl. Genia had dark hair. But then, on the other hand, Abus had blond hair too, and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Abus was Rozia's sin-- son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Son, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so she's the one who died, committed suicide, on the death march. Now, just let me take you back. Your father now comes-- under the prodding of your aunt-- comes to Czestochowa. And what part of year is that in '46?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say it was probably May, June. It was summertime. That, I remember.
